properties.title: Alameda
properties.description:
Key Vulnerabilities
  • Sea level rise threatens the entirety of Alameda’s shoreline and Bay Farm Island
  • Groundwater rise may impact legacy contamination at Alameda Point
Highlights
  • The Oakland Alameda Adaptation Committee (OAAC) is creating a plan to protect the city for up to 5.5ft of sea level rise
Opportunities
  • Continue to advance the long-term shoreline adaptation plan and the associated near-term projects. This will preserve community resources like the Robert W Crown Memorial Beach, new development on Alameda Point, and economic centers like the South Shore Shopping Center.
  • Ensure the storm drain system can function under future increased precipitation conditions and the impacts of sea level rise and groundwater rise.

properties.title: Brisbane
properties.description:
Key Vulnerabilities
  • Downtown Brisbane, Northwest Bayshore, and Tunnel Ave are vulnerable to groundwater rise and sea level rise
  • The Brisbane Baylands will be inundated by sea level rise
Highlights
  • The Brisbane Lagoon Watershed Sea Level Rise Adaptation Plan
Opportunities
  • Adopt OneShoreline’s guidelines for a Sea Level Rise Overlay District to ensure new development is resilient in the face of future flooding.
  • Update the Storm Drain Master Plan to ensure Brisbane’s storm drain infrastructure can function under future flood conditions.

properties.title: Burlingame
properties.description:
Key Vulnerabilities
  • Flooding in most of the Bayfront Area and parts of Rollins Road
Highlights
  • The Sea Level Rise Overlay District which establishes public access and sea level rise building standards for new developments
Opportunities
  • Establish building standards to protect against groundwater rise – such as requiring developments near contaminated sites to establish contaminant monitoring plans.
  • Update the Storm Drain Master Plan to ensure that the storm drain system can function under future climate conditions.

properties.title: Corte Madera
properties.description:
Key Vulnerabilities
  • Severe flooding in the Mariner Cove and Marina Village neighborhoods
  • Permanent inundation of marshes in the Corte Madera Ecological Reserve
Highlights
  • Regulations making it easier for homeowners to raise their homes above expected flood levels
Opportunities
  • Establish design standards to ensure new developments and infrastructure are resilient to future flood risk.
  • Maximize opportunities for nature-based flood resilience and marsh restoration in the city's ongoing flood berm upgrade planning.

properties.title: East Palo Alto
properties.description:
Key Vulnerabilities
  • Flooding along San Francisquito Creek and the shoreline
  • Contaminated sites along the shoreline
Highlights
  • The SAFER Bay Project, designed by the San Francisquito Creek JPA, will guard against 3.5 feet of sea level rise
Opportunities
  • Establish building design standards in at-risk areas to ensure new buildings are flood-resilient and to protect renters and homeowners in disadvantaged communities.
  • Work with community-based organizations and regulatory agencies to support the cleanup of contaminated sites. Community groups like PACT, YUCA, and Climate Resilient Communities are actively calling for the cleanup of specific sites due to the threat of future flooding.

properties.title: Emeryville
properties.description:
Key Vulnerabilities
  • Flooding on Powell Street, cutting off emergency routes, and flooding of commercial and industrial areas
  • Permanent inundation of salt marshes
Highlights
  • The East Bay Crescent Shoreline Project, which will include an ecotone levee in the Emeryville Crescent Marsh to protect the city from 5.5 ft of sea level rise
Opportunities
  • Develop strategies to address groundwater rise impacts on shoreline projects, vulnerable neighborhoods and infrastructure, the storm drain system, and contaminated sites on the shoreline.
  • Establish resilient design standards to ensure new developments and infrastructure are resilient to future flood risk.

properties.title: Hayward
properties.description:
Key Vulnerabilities
  • Flooding of industrial, commercial, and residential areas on the shoreline and along creeks and drainageways
  • Flooding of wetlands like the Oro Loma Marsh and Hayward Marsh
Highlights
  • The Hayward Regional Shoreline Adaptation Master Plan (HRSAP), which recommends nature-based strategies for preparing for sea level rise
  • The First Mile Horizontal Levee flood resilience and water treatment project
Opportunities
  • Establish design standards to ensure new developments and infrastructure are resilient to future flood risk.
  • Create a Storm Drain Master Plan that ensures that the city-owned portion of the storm drain system has the capacity to function under future flood conditions.

properties.title: Hercules
properties.description:
Key Vulnerabilities
  • Increased flooding along Pinole Creek and Refugio Creek
  • Sea level rise risk to the Hercules Hub, a planned multi-use development
Highlights
  • The Refugio Creek Overlay Zone, which preserves the land near Refugio Creek for flood protection and open space
Opportunities
  • Consider multiple sea level rise scenarios in the city’s resilience planning, including the possibility of 6.6 feet of sea level rise and related groundwater rise by 2100.
  • Establish resilient design standards to ensure new developments and infrastructure are resilient to future flood risk.

properties.title: Mill Valley
properties.description:
Key Vulnerabilities
  • More severe and frequent flooding in Shelter Bay and Tam Junction
  • Permanent inundation of Bothin Marsh
Highlights
  • The Mill Valley Shoreline Adaptation Framework will be a road map for protecting residents from the impacts of future flooding.
Opportunities
  • Implement a Sea Level Rise Overlay District to ensure any new development is able to withstand sea level rise and related flood risks.
  • Update the Storm Drain Master Plan to ensure Mill Valley’s storm drain infrastructure can function under future flood conditions.

properties.title: Novato
properties.description:
Key Vulnerabilities
  • Permanent inundation of the Hamilton Wetlands
  • More frequent and severe flooding of the Oaks Shopping Center and neighborhoods like Hamilton and Bahia
Highlights
  • Novato’s comprehensive vulnerability analysis that can be used to inform future sea level rise planning
Opportunities
  • Adopt a Sea Level Rise Overlay District to ensure any new development is built to withstand inundation from sea level rise, stormwater flooding, and emergent groundwater.
  • Create a Green Infrastructure Plan with a framework for multi-benefit green stormwater infrastructure implementation to improve flood control and water quality.

properties.title: Oakland
properties.description:
Key Vulnerabilities
  • Flooding in low-income neighborhoods and communities of color in the flatlands of West and East Oakland
  • Flooding of contaminated sites in West and East Oakland
Highlights
  • An updated Storm Drain Master Plan in progress to ensure the storm drain system can accommodate more extreme storms
  • The Oakland Alameda Adaptation Committee (OAAC), which is creating a plan to protect the Oakland-Alameda Estuary shoreline from up to 5.5ft of sea level rise
Opportunities
  • Establish design standards to ensure new developments and infrastructure are resilient to future flood risk.
  • Work with community members and regulatory agencies to prioritize and support the cleanup of contaminated sites that may pose a health risk to vulnerable communities when groundwater levels rise.

properties.title: Palo Alto
properties.description:
Key Vulnerabilities
  • Regular flooding of 4,000 homes and 14 miles of evacuation routes
  • Permanent inundation of wetlands in the Palo Alto Baylands
Highlights
  • The Palo Alto Living Levee Project, which will provide habitat, flood protection, and water quality benefits
Opportunities
  • Establish design standards to ensure new developments and infrastructure are resilient to future flood risk.
  • Monitor contaminated sites to ensure that pollutants are not travelling into nearby neighborhoods through sewer pipes.

properties.title: Pinole
properties.description:
Key Vulnerabilities
  • Flooding in neighborhoods along Pinole Creek
Highlights
  • The Pinole Creek Greenway Master Plan, which describes multi-benefit flood resilience and habitat restoration projects
Opportunities
  • Ensure that flood control plans for Pinole Creek account for future storms, which may become more frequent and extreme due to climate change.
  • Consider partnering with the City of Hercules to move forward with the Chelsea Wetlands restoration project, which is currently on hold.

properties.title: Redwood City
properties.description:
Key Vulnerabilities
  • Inundation of coastal developments like Redwood Shores
  • Flooding of Bair Island
Highlights
  • Redwood City’s Sea Level Rise Vulnerability Assessment and participation in OneShoreline, a collaborative effort between all San Mateo County cities to plan for sea level rise.
Opportunities
  • Utilize nature-based solutions as a part of the upcoming Redwood Shores Sea Level Rise Protection Project.
  • Update the Storm Drain Master Plan to ensure that Redwood City’s storm drain infrastructure can function under future flood conditions.

properties.title: San Carlos
properties.description:
Key Vulnerabilities
  • Flooding along Pulgas Creek, Cordilleras Creek, and Belmont Creek
  • Flooding of shoreline assets including residential neighborhoods, Highway 101, and the San Carlos Airport
Highlights
  • San Carlos has committed to creating its own Regional Shoreline Adaptation Plan (RSAP) to comply with state mandates.
Opportunities
  • Establish resilient design standards to ensure new developments and infrastructure are resilient to future flood risk. These could expand on existing sea level rise design standards for the Northeast Area neighborhood.
  • Study which areas will flood more frequently due to future storms and update the city’s flood resilience plans accordingly.

properties.title: San Jose
properties.description:
Key Vulnerabilities
  • Flooding in the Alviso neighborhood
  • Flooding along the Guadalupe River and Coyote Creek
Highlights
  • The Climate Adaptation and Resilience Plan, which is being created and will outline strategies to reduce the risk of climate-related disasters
Opportunities
  • Identify areas at risk of flooding from groundwater rise and integrate this analysis into the city’s ongoing flood protection projects.
  • Continue participating in the “Healthy Watersheds Thriving Communities” initiative, which is working to identify multi-benefit, community-informed green infrastructure projects. Once priority projects are identified, focus on funding and building these projects.

properties.title: San Mateo
properties.description:
Key Vulnerabilities
  • Flooding of 21 miles of highway, 83 miles of local roads at risk, 68 acres of wetlands, and homes and businesses
  • Flooding of contaminated sites near the shoreline
Highlights
  • Levee improvements to protect the North Shoreview neighborhood
  • Complete green street projects like the Laurel Elementary Safe Green Streets project and the Humboldt Sustainable Street improvements
Opportunities
  • Develop strategies to address groundwater rise impacts on shoreline projects, vulnerable neighborhoods and infrastructure, the storm drain system, and contaminated sites.
  • Establish resilient design standards to ensure new developments and infrastructure are resilient to future flood risk.

properties.title: Santa Clara
properties.description:
Key Vulnerabilities
  • Flooding of low-lying areas in northern Santa Clara near Highway 237
  • Flooding in neighborhoods along San Tomas Aquino Creek
Highlights
  • Santa Clara plans to join Santa Clara County’s countywide regional shoreline adaptation planning effort.
Opportunities
  • Consider multiple sea level rise scenarios in the city’s Regional Shoreline Adaptation Plan, including the possibility of 6.6 feet of sea level rise by 2100.
  • Update the city’s storm drain and flood resilience plans to ensure it can withstand future storm conditions.

properties.title: Sausalito
properties.description:
Key Vulnerabilities
  • Frequent flooding in Downtown Sausalito
  • Permanent inundation of transportation infrastructure like the ferry terminal, bus stops, and Bridgeway street
Highlights
  • Sausalito’s Shoreline Adaptation Plan, which plans for groundwater rise, stormwater, and sea level rise flooding
Opportunities
  • Adopt a Sea Level Rise Overlay District to ensure new development is resilient in the face of future flooding.
  • Create a Green Infrastructure Plan to integrate nature-based flood solutions into city infrastructure planning and meet flood resilience goals.

properties.title: South San Francisco
properties.description:
Key Vulnerabilities
  • Flooding of the Lindenville neighborhood and areas along Colma Creek
  • Flooding of the South San Francisco - San Bruno Water Quality Control Plant
Highlights
  • The Sea Level Rise Overlay District which establishes public access and sea level rise building standards for new developments
Opportunities
  • Establish a Groundwater Rise Overlay District to build upon the Sea Level Rise Overlay District and ensure that residents are protected from future emergent groundwater threats.
  • Update the city’s Storm Drain Master Plan to ensure that the storm drain infrastructure can function under future flood conditions.

properties.title: Sunnyvale
properties.description:
Key Vulnerabilities
  • Flooding in the Moffet Park neighborhood
  • Flooding of city’s wastewater treatment plant
Highlights
  • The Ecological Innovation District, an area of the Moffet Park neighborhood designated for sustainable and ecologically resilient development practices
Opportunities
  • Establish resilient design standards to ensure new developments and infrastructure are resilient to future flood risk. These can expand upon existing design standards in the Ecological Innovation District.
  • Develop an equity framework that identifies areas of overlapping vulnerabilities (such as extreme heat, flooding, and socioeconomic vulnerabilities) to determine where to prioritize climate adaptations.

properties.title: Tiburon
properties.description:
Key Vulnerabilities
  • Flooding of the ferry terminal, Bay Trail, and Highway 101
  • Flooding of Tiburon’s fire department, library, and post office
Highlights
  • The Sea Level Rise and Shoreline Adaptation Plan, which plans to protect against 6.5 feet of sea level rise
Opportunities
  • Adopt a Sea Level Rise Overlay District to ensure new development is resilient in the face of future flooding.
  • Update the Storm Drain Master Plan to ensure Tiburon’s storm drain infrastructure can function under future flood conditions.

properties.title: Union City
properties.description:
Key Vulnerabilities
  • Flooding in industrial, commercial, and residential areas along the shoreline and creek channels
  • Flooding of the Alvarado Wastewater Treatment Plant
Highlights
  • The city is updating its Climate Action Plan, which will include sea level rise adaptation strategies.
Opportunities
  • Partner with the neighboring cities of Fremont and Newark to create a Regional Shoreline Adaptation Plan to comply with state mandates.
  • Establish building design standards in at-risk areas to ensure new buildings are flood resilient.
